images.caption: Figure 1:Overview of SEAOTTER design and workflow.

Figure 2:(a) Classification accuracy and (b) CPU encoding throughput vs. on-device compression ratio. Shaded regions mark the compression ratio and throughput needed for 1080p/30 over Wi-Fi (25 Mbps), 720p/30 over 5G (5 Mbps), and 480p/30 over BLE (1 Mbps).■\blacksquare,>>, and≫\ggmark poor, fair, and excellent on-device suitability; configurations in the red region are poorly suited.
Figure 3:JPEG workflow with learned color transform and quantization and visualization of companding/decompanding functions. Dotted lines indicate the signal path during training.
Figure 4:Rate–distortion–accuracy trade-offs vs. transmit compression ratio: (top) SSIM and DISTS; (bottom) ADE20K mIoU and SigLIP-2 zero-shot top-1. SEAOTTER-ZS leads the perceptual-quality axes.
Figure 5:Performance trade-offs of SEAOTTER variants vs other codecs.

Figure 6:Learned per-rate DCT-domain quantization matricesQ(k)Q^{(k)}fork=0,1,2k=0,1,2(top row) alongside the matched-bpp ITU T.81 4:4:4 quantization tables (bottom row). Per-channel colormap hues are derived fromℱ\mathcal{F}’s learned RGB-mixing kernel; the resulting color space coincides with YCgCo up to per-channel sign.

Figure 7:Standalone learned JPEG codec versus ITU T.81 (with and without chroma subsampling) on the Kodak validation set at native resolution. SEAOTTER’s three trained operating points (k∈{0,1,2}k\in\{0,1,2\}) dominate matched-bpp ITU T.81 4:4:4 by+0.27+0.27/+1.40+1.40/+1.27+1.27dB in PSNR.

Figure 8:Top row (a–c): downstream task accuracy vs. thestoragecompression ratio (on-disk JPEG size after transcode). Bottom row (d–f): sensor-embedded transmit CR vs. downstream consumer CR, with ay=xy{=}xreference line.
